questionText: Prove that $n^2 - n$ is even for every integer $n$.

steps.explanation: We start from the expression to be shown even and look for structure.
Taking out $n$ rewrites it as a product of two integers.
$n-1$ and $n$ follow one after another, so exactly one of them is even.
If $n$ is even, the factor $n$ carries a $2$, so the product is even.
If $n$ is odd, then $n-1$ is even, so again the product is even.
In both cases the product equals twice an integer, hence even.

steps.workingLatex: n^2 - n \text{ is even for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}
steps.explanation: Since $n^2-n=n(n-1)$ is a product of consecutive integers, it is always even.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $n^2-n=n(n-1)$ is a product of consecutive integers, one of which is even, so it is even for every integer $n$. $\blacksquare$
